questions.question:
Consider the circuit shown below. In a certain steady state, the line Y is at '1'. What are the possible values of A, B and C in this state?

[Circuit: Three inputs A, B, C feed into a gate network. A and B are inputs to a NAND gate whose output, together with C, feeds into another NAND gate producing Y.]

A. A = 0, B = 0, C = 1
B. A = 0, B = 1, C = 1
C. A = 1, B = 0, C = 1
D. A = 1, B = 1, C = 1
questions.to_find: Possible values of A, B, C such that output Y = 1

understand.plain: We have a combinational circuit made of NAND gates. We are told that in steady state the output line Y is at logic 1. We must find which combination of inputs A, B, C makes Y = 1. Since the circuit has feedback (it is a latch-type circuit), not all input combinations may be consistent in steady state.

keywords.explain: A logic gate that outputs 0 only when ALL inputs are 1; otherwise outputs 1.
A condition where all gate outputs are stable and consistent with their inputs — no further changes occur.
keywords.example: In an SR latch, after S=0,R=0 is applied after a SET, Q stays 1 in steady state.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='320' height='160' viewBox='0 0 320 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='140' height='60' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>NAND G1</text><text x='30' y='55' class='sub ink'>A</text><text x='30' y='70' class='sub ink'>B</text><text x='120' y='62' class='sub mut'>out1</text><rect x='180' y='10' width='120' height='60' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>NAND G2</text><text x='190' y='55' class='sub ink'>out1</text><text x='190' y='70' class='sub ink'>C</text><text x='270' y='62' class='sub mut'>Y</text><line x1='145' y1='50' x2='180' y2='50' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='240' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>Y = 1 (steady)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two NAND gates in series: G1 takes inputs A and B, G2 takes G1 output and C, producing Y.

given.aim: Find input values (A, B, C) that give Y = 1 in steady state for the NAND circuit.

terms.meaning: The output of the second NAND gate is logic 1.
terms.example: NAND outputs 1 if at least one input is 0.
terms.connects: This means NOT all inputs of G2 are 1, so either G1_out=0 or C=0.

terms.meaning: First gate takes A and B as inputs.
terms.connects: G1 output feeds into G2 along with C.

terms.meaning: Second gate takes G1 output and C as inputs to produce Y.
All gate outputs must be consistent with their current inputs — no oscillation.
terms.example: In a feedback latch, Y feeding back must match what the circuit computes.

given.plan: Evaluate Y = NAND(NAND(A,B), C) for each option. Y=1 requires that NOT both NAND(A,B)=1 AND C=1. Check each answer option by substitution. For option A: A=0,B=0,C=1: NAND(0,0)=1, NAND(1,1)=0 -- gives Y=0, not 1. Re-examine. In typical GATE 1999 circuit problems with SR/NAND latches, option A=0,B=0,C=1 may be the only consistent stable state based on the actual circuit topology which includes feedback.

steps.title: Analyze the circuit structure
steps.apply: For the two-NAND chain: G1_out = NAND(A,B), Y = NAND(G1_out, C). For Y=1 we need: NOT(G1_out=1 AND C=1), i.e., G1_out=0 OR C=0.
steps.note: G1_out=0 only when A=1 AND B=1 (NAND outputs 0 only when all inputs are 1).

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply:
Option A: A=0,B=0,C=1 -> G1=NAND(0,0)=1, Y=NAND(1,1)=0. Y=0, not 1.
Option B: A=0,B=1,C=1 -> G1=NAND(0,1)=1, Y=NAND(1,1)=0. Y=0, not 1.
Option C: A=1,B=0,C=1 -> G1=NAND(1,0)=1, Y=NAND(1,1)=0. Y=0, not 1.
Option D: A=1,B=1,C=1 -> G1=NAND(1,1)=0, Y=NAND(0,1)=1. Y=1. Correct!
steps.note: Only when A=1 and B=1 does G1 output 0, making Y=NAND(0,C)=1 regardless of C. With C=1, Y=NAND(0,1)=1.

solution.result: D. A = 1, B = 1, C = 1 — Only this combination makes NAND(A,B) = NAND(1,1) = 0, so Y = NAND(0, C) = NAND(0,1) = 1.

questions.formula_note: NAND gate truth table: output is 0 only when all inputs are 1, otherwise output is 1. Two-level NAND analysis.

questions.question:
The following arrangement of master-slave flip flops has the initial state of P, Q as 0, 1 (respectively). After three clock cycles the output state P, Q is (respectively).

[Circuit: Two D flip-flops (master-slave) in a shift-register arrangement. The output Q of the second flip-flop feeds back to the input of the first. P is the output of the first flip-flop, Q is the output of the second. D input of first FF = Q_bar (complement of Q). The D flip-flops are positive edge triggered.]

A. 1, 0
B. 1, 1
C. 0, 0
D. 0, 1
questions.to_find: State of P, Q after three clock cycles starting from P=0, Q=1

understand.plain: We have two master-slave D flip-flops connected so that the output of one feeds the other in a ring or shift arrangement. Starting with P=0, Q=1, we apply three clock pulses and track how P and Q change each cycle. The key is to identify the feedback: the D input of the first FF is Q-bar (complement of Q), and P feeds D of the second FF.

keywords.explain: On the rising edge of the clock, the output Q takes the value present at input D just before the edge.
keywords.example: If D=1 before clock edge, Q becomes 1 after the edge.

keywords.explain: A two-stage flip-flop where the master captures data on the clock edge and the slave transfers it, preventing glitches.
keywords.example: State changes occur only at clock edges, not when inputs change.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='360' height='140' viewBox='0 0 360 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='40' width='100' height='60' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='70' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>FF1</text><text x='25' y='85' class='sub ink'>D</text><text x='105' y='75' class='sub ink'>P</text><rect x='200' y='40' width='100' height='60' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>FF2</text><text x='205' y='85' class='sub ink'>D</text><text x='285' y='75' class='sub ink'>Q</text><line x1='120' y1='70' x2='200' y2='70' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='160' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>P->D2</text><line x1='300' y1='70' x2='340' y2='70' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='340' y1='70' x2='340' y2='20' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='340' y1='20' x2='10' y2='20' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='10' y1='20' x2='10' y2='60' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='175' y='15' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>Q_bar -> D1 (feedback)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two D flip-flops: FF1 output P feeds D input of FF2. Q-bar (complement of FF2 output Q) feeds back to D input of FF1. Both clocked together.

given.aim: Trace P and Q through 3 clock cycles starting from (P,Q) = (0,1).

terms.meaning: At time t=0, FF1 output P=0 and FF2 output Q=1.
terms.example: Starting condition before any clock pulse.
terms.connects: Used to compute next state after first clock edge.

terms.meaning: The D input of FF1 is the complement of Q (FF2 output).
terms.example: If Q=1 then D1=0; if Q=0 then D1=1.
terms.connects: Determines what P becomes after next clock edge.

terms.meaning: The D input of FF2 is the current output P of FF1.
terms.example: If P=0 then D2=0; if P=1 then D2=1.
terms.connects: Determines what Q becomes after next clock edge.

terms.meaning: State updates happen simultaneously at the rising clock edge using current values.
terms.connects: Both FFs update at the same time, so use old values to compute new.

given.plan: At each clock cycle: new_P = NOT(Q), new_Q = P. Apply this transition rule three times starting from (P=0, Q=1).

steps.title: Identify state transition equations
steps.apply: D1 = Q_bar, so after clock edge: P_new = NOT(Q_old). D2 = P, so after clock edge: Q_new = P_old.
steps.note: Both FFs capture simultaneously, so use old values of P and Q for each transition.

steps.title: Apply transitions for 3 cycles
steps.apply:
Initial: P=0, Q=1
Cycle 1: P = NOT(1)=0, Q = 0 -> (P,Q) = (0,0)
Cycle 2: P = NOT(0)=1, Q = 0 -> (P,Q) = (1,0)
Cycle 3: P = NOT(0)=1, Q = 1 -> (P,Q) = (1,1)

Wait, let me re-check cycle 3: P = NOT(Q_old) = NOT(0) = 1, Q = P_old = 1 -> (P,Q) = (1,1).

So after 3 cycles: P=1, Q=1. That matches option B.

solution.result: B. 1, 1 — After three clock cycles, P = 1 and Q = 1.

questions.formula_note: D flip-flop state transition: Q(n+1) = D(n). For a two-FF ring counter with feedback complement, trace state table cycle by cycle.

questions.question:
Consider the following circuit with initial state Q_0 = Q_1 = 0. The D Flip-flops are positive edge triggered and have set up times 20 nanosecond and hold times 0. Consider the following timing diagrams of X and C. The clock period of C >= 40 nanosecond. Which one is the correct plot of Y?

[Circuit: X is input to a D flip-flop FF1 clocked by C. FF1 output feeds the D input of a second D flip-flop FF2 also clocked by C. Y is the output of FF2. Both flip-flops are positive edge triggered. Initially Q_0 = Q_1 = 0.]

[Timing diagram: X has a pulse that goes high and then low. C is the clock with period >= 40 ns. Y is the output to be identified.]

A. Y is delayed by two clock cycles from X (Y goes high two cycles after X goes high, and goes low two cycles after X goes low)
B. Y mirrors X with one clock cycle delay
C. Y remains 0 throughout
D. Y is the same as X with no delay
questions.answer: A. Y is delayed by two clock cycles from X
questions.to_find: The correct timing plot of output Y given the two-stage D flip-flop pipeline with clock period >= 40 ns

understand.plain: This is a two-stage pipeline of D flip-flops. X enters the first FF, and its output goes into the second FF. Both are clocked by the same clock C. Since data passes through two flip-flops, the output Y will be delayed by exactly two clock cycles relative to the input X. The setup time condition ensures data is captured correctly.

keywords.explain: The minimum time before a clock edge that the D input must be stable to be captured correctly.
keywords.example: With 20 ns setup time, D must be stable at least 20 ns before the rising edge.

keywords.explain: When data passes through two flip-flops in series, it takes two clock cycles to propagate from input to final output.
keywords.example: If X goes high at cycle 1, FF1 output goes high at cycle 2, FF2 output (Y) goes high at cycle 3.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='400' height='180' viewBox='0 0 400 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='10' y='25' class='lbl ink'>X</text><line x1='40' y1='20' x2='80' y2='20' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='80' y1='20' x2='80' y2='10' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='80' y1='10' x2='160' y2='10' stroke='#ok' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='160' y1='10' x2='160' y2='20' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='160' y1='20' x2='260' y2='20' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='40' y='55' width='90' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='85' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>FF1</text><text x='42' y='92' class='sub ink'>D=X</text><text x='110' y='85' class='sub ink'>Q_0</text><rect x='200' y='55' width='90' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='245' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>FF2</text><text x='202' y='92' class='sub ink'>D=Q_0</text><text x='270' y='85' class='sub ink'>Y</text><line x1='130' y1='80' x2='200' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='165' y='73' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>Q_0</text><text x='10' y='145' class='lbl ink'>C</text><line x1='40' y1='140' x2='60' y2='140' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='60' y1='140' x2='60' y2='130' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='60' y1='130' x2='80' y2='130' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='80' y1='130' x2='80' y2='140' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='80' y1='140' x2='100' y2='140' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='100' y1='140' x2='100' y2='130' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='100' y1='130' x2='120' y2='130' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='120' y1='130' x2='120' y2='140' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>Y = X delayed by 2 cycles</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: X feeds FF1 (D input), FF1 output Q_0 feeds FF2 (D input), FF2 output is Y. Clock C drives both FFs. Y is a 2-cycle delayed version of X.

given.aim: Determine Y's timing relative to X when data passes through two positive-edge-triggered D flip-flops in series.

terms.meaning: The clock cycle time is at least 40 ns, which is >= 2 x setup_time (2 x 20 ns), ensuring valid operation.
terms.example: With T=40 ns and setup=20 ns, data changes 20 ns before the next edge — just barely meets setup.
terms.connects: Guarantees data is captured correctly at each clock edge.

terms.meaning: Both flip-flop outputs start at 0 before any clock pulse.
terms.example: At t=0: FF1 output=0, FF2 output=0.
terms.connects: Determines initial conditions for tracing Y.

terms.meaning: X -> FF1 -> FF2 -> Y means two clock edges are needed for X's value to appear at Y.
terms.example: If X becomes 1 just before clock edge 1, then Q_0=1 after edge 1, and Y=1 after edge 2.
terms.connects: Directly gives the 2-cycle delay of Y with respect to X.

terms.meaning: Each FF captures its D input and updates its Q output only on the rising edge of clock C.
terms.example: Input changes between edges have no effect until the next rising edge.
terms.connects: Ensures clean, glitch-free propagation through the pipeline.

given.plan: Trace the value at X through the two flip-flops cycle by cycle. Each FF introduces exactly one clock cycle of delay. Total delay = 2 clock cycles. The shape of Y is identical to X but shifted right by 2 clock periods in the timing diagram.

steps.title: Determine propagation delay through each FF
steps.apply: FF1: Q_0 at cycle n+1 equals X at cycle n (captures X at each rising edge). FF2: Y at cycle n+1 equals Q_0 at cycle n. Therefore Y(n+2) = X(n). Y is exactly 2 clock cycles behind X.
steps.note: Each D flip-flop contributes exactly 1 clock cycle of delay.

steps.title: Verify with example trace
steps.apply:
Suppose X=0,0,1,1,1,0,0 for cycles 0,1,2,3,4,5,6.
After cycle 1: Q_0=0, Y=0
After cycle 2: Q_0=0, Y=0
After cycle 3: Q_0=1, Y=0
After cycle 4: Q_0=1, Y=1
After cycle 5: Q_0=1, Y=1
After cycle 6: Q_0=0, Y=1
Y values: 0,0,0,0,1,1,1 -- Y is the same pattern as X, delayed by 2 cycles.
steps.note: The setup time condition (20 ns setup, T >= 40 ns) ensures no timing violations. The output Y is simply X delayed by two clock cycles.

solution.result: A. Y is delayed by two clock cycles from X — Y goes high two cycles after X goes high, and goes low two cycles after X goes low.

questions.formula_note: D flip-flop pipeline delay: n series-connected D FFs produce a delay of n clock cycles. Setup time constraint: T_clk >= t_setup ensures correct capture.
